---
title: "5.3.1 Cyber security threats"
subtitle: "DDoS attacks and hacking · P190-P191"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.72em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.34em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)

## DoS Attacks

### Preventing access

A <span class="term">denial of service (DoS) attack</span> is an attempt at preventing users from accessing part of a network, notably an internet server.

This is usually temporary but may be a damaging act or a large breach of security.

## What Attackers Prevent

### Network services

An attacker may be able to prevent a user from:

::: {.tight-list}
- accessing their emails
- accessing websites or web pages
- accessing online services, such as banking
:::

## Flooding with Spam Traffic

### Overloading the server

One method of attack is to flood the network with useless <span class="term">spam traffic</span>.

When a server becomes overloaded by an attacker sending thousands of requests, it cannot service a user's legitimate request. This is effectively a denial of service.

## Distributed DoS

### Many different computers

In a <span class="term">distributed denial of service (DDoS)</span> attack, the spam traffic originates from many different computers.

This makes it hard to block the attack.

DDoS can also target an email account by clogging it with thousands of spam messages.

## Guarding Against DDoS

### Textbook mitigations

An individual user or website can guard against these attacks to some degree by:

::: {.tight-list}
- using an up-to-date malware checker
- setting up a firewall to restrict traffic to and from the web server or user's computer
- applying email filters to filter out unwanted traffic such as spam
:::

## Signs of DDoS

### Symptoms to watch for

There are signs a user can look out for:

::: {.tight-list}
- slow network performance, such as opening files or accessing websites
- inability to access certain websites
- large amounts of spam email reaching the user's email account
:::

## Hacking

### Illegal access

<span class="term">Hacking</span> is generally the act of gaining illegal access to a computer system without the user's permission.

This can lead to <span class="mark">identity theft</span> or gaining personal information; data can be deleted, passed on, changed or corrupted.

## Encryption and Hacking

### What encryption does not do

Encryption does not stop hacking.

It makes the data meaningless to the hacker, but it does not stop them from deleting, corrupting or passing on the data.

## Preventing Hacking

### Security controls

Hacking can be prevented through the use of:

::: {.tight-list}
- firewalls
- user names
- frequently changed strong passwords
- anti-hacking software
- intrusion-detection software
:::

## Ethical Hacking

### Authorised testing

Malicious hacking takes place without the user's permission and is always illegal.

<span class="term">Ethical hacking</span> occurs when companies authorise paid hackers to check their security measures and test how robust their computer systems are to hacking attacks.

## Classroom Check

### DDoS and hacking

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- DoS prevents users from accessing part of a network or online service
- DDoS uses spam traffic from many different computers
- symptoms include slow network performance, inaccessible websites and large spam email volumes
- firewalls, malware checkers and email filters reduce risk
- hacking is illegal access without permission
- encryption does not stop hacking; strong passwords, user names, firewalls and intrusion detection help prevent it
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Malware

The next deck begins the six malware types listed in Figure 5.10.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)
